lenses.guidance: vv 14-20 form an ironic king-charter: must not multiply horses, wives, gold; must write a copy of the Torah and read it daily. The structure inverts ancient-Near-East kingship — the king is Torah's servant, not its master.

vv 16-17 forbid horses, wives, and gold; 1 Kgs 10:14-11:8 records Solomon publicly violating all three. The canonical thread runs from Deuteronomy's prohibition to the chronicler's verdict and to Ps 72's righteous-king ideal.

vv 18-20 — the king must write a Torah-copy and read it daily — prefigures the King who is himself the Word (Jn 1:1, 14). Christ is the true Davidic ruler who not only obeys Torah but embodies and fulfills it.

vv 14-20 set up the covenant-king motif the redemption arc requires: a Davidic son who keeps the law of God. The promise running through 2 Sam 7 to Christ rests on this foundational charter — kings under Torah, never above.
